headline: Sarah’s performance story is strong technical output, but she under-weights influence and visibility by waiting to socialize direction until analysis is complete.
summary_bullets: Across multiple answers, Sarah ties her uneven influence to a consistent pattern: “wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction.”
She names the stakeholder symptom and the gap as one thread: late surprises create a need for “a tighter narrative on tradeoffs, not just stronger models.”
Her “next quarter focus” is process-heavy but still missing a concrete stakeholder signal plan: she chose a deliverable and “define what good looks like,” not how she will shape decisions earlier.
understanding_paragraph: We understood that Sarah can produce solid technical work, but her default operating mode delays decision-shaping until the analysis is finished. She also understands the stakeholder cost of that delay—“stakeholders feel surprised late”—and connects it to tradeoff communication. What we didn’t fully pin down is how she will operationalize early influence (timing, artifacts, and decision points) rather than only improving models and narratives after analysis.

axes.meaning: No wellbeing signal in this session; not actionable on its own.
Weak signal of engagement: she recognizes gaps and wants to level up, but doesn’t show strong initiative beyond intentions.
Clear gap in how she sets and communicates expectations with non-technical partners; priorities and decision framing are not landing early.
Slight negative growth signal: she identifies what to improve (tradeoff narrative, checkpoints) but the plan lacks concrete execution mechanics.

brutal_truth_employee: Sarah’s core pattern is: “I tend to wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction.” She also states the consequence plainly: “That slows trust because stakeholders feel surprised late.”
brutal_truth_manager: When Sarah said “I tend to wait until analysis is complete before socializing direction,” the conversation stayed at diagnosis and didn’t force a specific early-influence mechanism (what she will share, when, and with whom). Her answer “I will choose one recurring deliverable and define what good looks like” is a start, but it needs a stakeholder decision-timing commitment next time.

next_actions.action: Email Sarah a one-page template for “early influence” including decision points, tradeoff narrative bullets, and the exact stakeholder update cadence she will use this quarter.
Schedule a 30-minute working session with Sarah to pick one cross-team decision and draft the first stakeholder-facing narrative before analysis completes.
Open next 1:1 by asking Sarah to report: the first early update she sent, who it went to, what decision it shaped, and what she changed after feedback.

watch_for: Before next 1:1: if Sarah still describes her process as “wait until analysis is complete” without naming an earlier stakeholder update, treat clarity/influence as unchanged.
Within two weeks: if stakeholders continue to report “surprised late” outcomes, require Sarah to show the tradeoff narrative artifact and timing for the next decision.
